Our Lord Jesus, by telling his disciples about future trouble, wanted to prevent them from being shocked. Even people who seem dedicated to God's service can actually be enemies of it. This does not excuse the actions of those who persecute others; evil deeds will never be made right by claiming they are done for God. Just as Jesus endured suffering, so should his followers endure their own hardships, looking toward the fulfillment of Scripture. He did not tell them sooner because he was with them to teach, guide, and comfort them; they didn't need that promise of the Holy Spirit’s presence then. It is pointless to ask where troubles come from; it is better to ask where they go, since we know they work for good. A common mistake among Christians who are troubled is focusing only on the negative aspects and ignoring joy and gladness. Excessive affection for this life fills people's hearts with sorrow. The love of the world and its sorrows prevent us from finding joy in God.

Christ’s departure was necessary for the Comforter to come. Sending the Spirit was a result of Christ’s death, which involved his leaving. His physical presence could only be in one place at a time, but his Spirit is everywhere, in all places, at all times, whenever two or three are gathered together in his name. Here we see the Spirit's role: first to convict and then to comfort. Conviction is the Spirit’s work; he can do it effectively, and no one else can. The Holy Spirit works by first convincing people and then comforting them. The Spirit will convince the world of sin—not just tell them about it. The Spirit convinces people of the fact of sin, the fault of sin, the foolishness of sin, the filthiness of sin that makes us hateful to God, the source of sin (our corrupt nature), and finally, the consequence of sin: death. The Holy Spirit proves that everyone in the world is guilty before God. He convinces the world of righteousness—that Jesus of Nazareth was Christ, the righteous one. Also, he shows them Christ’s righteousness, which is given to us for justification and salvation. He will show them where it can be found and how they may be accepted as righteous in God's sight. Christ’s ascension proves that his ransom was accepted and his righteousness was completed, through which believers are justified. Of judgment, because the ruler of this world has been judged. All will go well when the one who caused all the trouble is defeated. As Satan is subdued by Christ, we have confidence, for no other power can stand against him. And of the day of judgment. The coming of the Spirit would be greatly beneficial to the disciples. The Holy Spirit guides us, not only showing us the way but also going with us through continued help and influence. To be led into a truth is more than just knowing it; it means having the understanding, appreciation, and power of it in your heart. He will teach all truth and withhold nothing that is helpful, because he will reveal things to come. All the gifts and graces of the Spirit, all the preaching and writing of the apostles, under the influence of the Spirit, all languages and miracles—all were meant to glorify Christ. Everyone should ask whether the Holy Spirit has begun a good work in their heart? Without a clear understanding of our guilt and danger, we will never understand the value of Christ’s salvation; but when we come to know ourselves correctly, we begin to see the value of the Redeemer. We would have a better understanding of the Redeemer and stronger feelings toward him if we prayed for and depended on the Holy Spirit more.

It is helpful to remember how quickly our opportunities for grace end, so that we can make the most of them. But the disciples’ sorrows will soon turn into joy, like those of a mother seeing her baby. The Holy Spirit would be their Comforter, and neither people nor demons, nor suffering in life or death, would ever take away their joy. Believers experience joy or sorrow depending on how they see Christ and feel his presence. Sorrow is coming to the ungodly, which nothing can lessen; believers inherit joy that no one can take away. Where now is the joy of those who murdered our Lord, and the sorrow of his friends?

Asking the Father shows an awareness of spiritual needs and a desire for spiritual blessings, with the belief that they come only from God. Asking in Christ’s name acknowledges our unworthiness to receive any favors from God and demonstrates complete dependence on Christ as our Righteousness. Our Lord had previously spoken in short, meaningful statements or parables, which the disciples did not fully understand, but after his resurrection he intended to plainly teach them about the Father and the way to him through his intercession. The fact that our Lord repeatedly encourages us to offer prayers in his name shows that a major purpose of Christ’s mediation is to impress upon us a deep sense of our sinfulness and the merit and power of his death, by which we have access to God. And let us always remember that addressing the Father in the name of Christ or addressing the Son as God dwelling in human nature and reconciling the world to himself are the same thing, because the Father and Son are one.

Here is a clear statement about Christ’s coming from the Father and his return to him. The Redeemer, when he came, was God made visible in human form, and upon his departure, he was received into glory. This saying helped the disciples grow in knowledge. They also grew in faith; “Now we are sure.” Sadly, they did not understand their own weakness. The divine nature did not abandon the human nature but supported it and gave value to Christ’s sufferings. And while we have God's favor, we are happy and should be at peace, even if the whole world rejects us. Peace in Christ is the only true peace; believers have it through him alone. Through him we have peace with God, and therefore we have peace in our own minds. We should feel encouraged because Christ has overcome the world before us. But while we think we are standing firm, let us be careful lest we fall. We do not know how we would act if faced with temptation; so let us watch and pray without ceasing that we may not be left to ourselves.
